name: Rumi
description: Verses of love and the longing for the divine from the Sufi poet Jalal al-Din Rumi.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: Listen to this reed how it complains, telling a tale of separations—
quotes.notes: The opening line of the Masnavi's 'Song of the Reed,' in Reynold A. Nicholson's scholarly prose translation (1925-1940).

quotes.content: Ever since I was parted from the reed-bed, man and woman have moaned in (unison with) my lament.
quotes.notes: The reed's cry of separation from its source; Nicholson's translation of the Masnavi's proem.

quotes.content: I want a bosom torn by severance, that I may unfold (to such a one) the pain of love-desire.
quotes.notes: From the 'Song of the Reed' proem; Nicholson's translation, with his parenthetical clarifications preserved.

quotes.content: Every one who is left far from his source wishes back the time when he was united with it.
quotes.notes: A core statement of the Masnavi's theme of separation and reunion; Nicholson's translation.

quotes.content: This noise of the reed is fire, it is not wind: whoso hath not this fire, may he be naught!
quotes.notes: The reed's sound as the fire of love; Nicholson's translation of the Masnavi's proem.

quotes.content: 'Tis the fire of Love that is in the reed, 'tis the fervour of Love that is in the wine.
quotes.notes: From the 'Song of the Reed'; Nicholson's translation of the Masnavi.

quotes.content: The reed is the comrade of every one who has been parted from a friend: its strains pierced our hearts.
quotes.notes: The reed as companion to all who have been separated; Nicholson's translation.

quotes.content: None that is raw understands the state of the ripe: therefore my words must be brief. Farewell!
quotes.notes: Closing line of the 'Song of the Reed' proem; Nicholson's translation of the Masnavi.

quotes.content: O son, burst thy chains and be free! How long wilt thou be a bondsman to silver and gold?
quotes.notes: An exhortation to spiritual freedom following the reed's song; Nicholson's translation.

quotes.content: If thou pour the sea into a pitcher, how much will it hold? One day's store.
quotes.notes: On the limits of worldly desire and vessels; Nicholson's translation of the Masnavi.

quotes.content: He (alone) whose garment is rent by a (mighty) love is purged entirely of covetousness and defect.
quotes.notes: On love's purifying power; Nicholson's translation, with his editorial parentheticals kept.

quotes.content: Hail, our sweet-thoughted Love—thou that art the physician of all our ills, the remedy of our pride and vainglory, our Plato and our Galen!
quotes.notes: A salutation to Love as healer; Nicholson's translation of the Masnavi's proem.

quotes.content: Through Love the earthly body soared to the skies: the mountain began to dance and became nimble.
quotes.notes: On love's transforming, elevating force; Nicholson's translation of the Masnavi.

quotes.content: Love inspired Mount Sinai, O lover, (so that) Sinai (was made) drunken and Moses fell in a swoon.
quotes.notes: Alluding to the Quranic theophany at Sinai; Nicholson's translation of the Masnavi.

quotes.content: Being in love is made manifest by soreness of heart: there is no sickness like heartsickness.
quotes.notes: On the ache that marks the true lover; Nicholson's translation of the Masnavi.

quotes.content: The lover's ailment is separate from all other ailments: love is the astrolabe of the mysteries of God.
quotes.notes: The famous image of love as an astrolabe reading the divine; Nicholson's translation.

quotes.content: Whatsoever I say in exposition and explanation of Love, when I come to Love (itself) I am ashamed of that (explanation).
quotes.notes: On the inadequacy of words before love; Nicholson's translation of the Masnavi.

quotes.content: Although the commentary of the tongue makes (all) clear, yet tongueless love is clearer.
quotes.notes: That silent love speaks more plainly than speech; Nicholson's translation of the Masnavi.

quotes.content: The elephant was in a dark house: some Hindus had brought it for exhibition.
quotes.notes: Opening of the parable of the elephant in the dark; Nicholson's translation of the Masnavi.

quotes.content: The eye of sense-perception is only like the palm of the hand: the palm hath not power to reach the whole of him.
quotes.notes: The moral of the elephant parable on the limits of perception; Nicholson's translation.

quotes.content: The eye of the Sea is one thing, and the foam another: leave the foam and look with the eye of the Sea.
quotes.notes: Following the elephant parable, on seeing past appearances; Nicholson's translation.

quotes.content: I died to the inorganic state and became endowed with growth, and (then) I died to (vegetable) growth and attained to the animal.
quotes.notes: Nicholson's exact prose of the celebrated 'evolution of the soul' passage, distinct from the loose popular 'I died as a mineral' verse rendering.

quotes.content: I died from animality and became Adam (man): why, then, should I fear? When have I become less by dying?
quotes.notes: On death as ascent rather than loss; Nicholson's translation of the Masnavi.
